Crickets hear with an organ called a tympanum on their front legs, not their head.
Only male crickets chirp. They rub their wings together to make that classic sound.
A cricket's chirp rate often rises with temperature—some people use this to estimate the air temp.
House crickets are famous for living near people, but thousands of wild species exist worldwide.
Crickets have long antennae (feelers) that help them smell and sense their surroundings at night.
Many crickets are nocturnal, which is why you hear them after dark.
Crickets have compound eyes with many tiny lenses, great for spotting movement in dim light.
Some crickets can jump many times their body length to escape predators in a split second.
Field crickets often live in grass and soil, not just inside your kitchen.
Crickets breathe through tiny holes called spiracles along the sides of their body.
Baby crickets are called nymphs and look like tiny adults without wings at first.
Crickets go through incomplete metamorphosis: egg, nymph, adult—no caterpillar-like stage.
Camel crickets get their name from a humped back; they often live in damp basements.
Mole crickets have sturdy front legs built for digging tunnels underground.
Tree crickets are often bright green and blend in with leaves while they sing.
Snowy tree crickets are sometimes called the thermometer cricket because their song tracks heat.
Jerusalem crickets look bulky and odd, but they are not true crickets—they are a different insect group.
True crickets belong to the family Gryllidae in the order Orthoptera with grasshoppers.
Some ground crickets make soft, high-pitched trills that are easy to miss unless you listen closely.
Crickets use their cerci—tail-like sensors—to feel vibrations in the ground or air.
In many cultures, crickets are considered good luck if they move into a home.
Crickets are omnivores: they may eat plant bits, decaying matter, and sometimes other small insects.
A quiet winter night has fewer cricket songs in cold regions because many species die off or go dormant.
Some pet reptiles are fed farm-raised crickets because they are high in protein.
Crickets have exoskeletons made of chitin that they shed as they grow larger.
After a bad molt, a cricket might end up with a bent wing—but it can often still chirp.
Bush crickets might sound like cousins, but in everyday talk people often mix them up with true crickets.
Sticky traps can catch stray house crickets, but sealing gaps helps keep them out in the first place.
Crickets can sense humidity and often prefer cooler, sheltered spots during hot afternoons.
If two male crickets meet, sometimes they chirp competitively like a tiny volume contest.
